summary:
The division of the [[event:81|Roman Empire]] into Eastern and Western halves was not a single event but a process that solidified over the 4th century, culminating in the permanent split at the death of Emperor Theodosius I in 395 AD. The empire had been ruled by multiple co-emperors since Diocletian's tetrarchy (284 AD), reflecting the practical impossibility of a single ruler governing a territory stretching from Scotland to Mesopotamia. Constantine reunified the empire but appointed co-rulers; after his death it was repeatedly divided among his sons and successors. Theodosius I was the last emperor to rule a genuinely united empire, and at his death he assigned the West to his son Honorius and the East to Arcadius.

The Eastern and Western empires diverged rapidly in subsequent decades. The East — centered on Constantinople, controlling the wealthy provinces of Anatolia, Egypt, Syria, and Greece — was economically stronger, more urbanized, and better able to defend itself. The West, controlling poorer agricultural provinces increasingly vulnerable to Germanic pressure, spiraled into financial and military crisis. The West fell definitively in 476 AD when Odoacer deposed the last Western emperor Romulus Augustulus ([[event:2861|Odoacer deposes Romulus]]); the Eastern Empire continued as the Byzantine Empire for another thousand years, finally falling to the Ottoman Turks in 1453. The permanent division of the empire — Latin West versus Greek East, Catholic versus Orthodox Christianity, Germanic kingdoms versus Byzantine continuity — shaped the entire subsequent history of Europe and the Mediterranean world.

The divergence between East and West had cultural and religious dimensions as deep as the political. Western Latin Christianity (eventually Catholicism and Protestantism) and Eastern Greek Christianity (Orthodoxy) reflect the two halves of the divided empire; the boundary between Catholic and Orthodox Europe today closely tracks the old border between the Western and Eastern Roman Empires.
